engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes-Benz C140 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the C140 coupé specifically — across the full 1992–1999 production run, from the entry-level 4.2-litre V8 to the range-topping 6.0-litre V12.

overview.intro: The Mercedes-Benz C140 is the two-door coupé variant of the W140 S-Class, unveiled alongside the saloon at the Geneva Motor Show in March 1991. Production of the coupé began in October 1992, with the C140 initially badged as the SEC (e.g., 500 SEC, 600 SEC), renamed the S-Class Coupé from 1993, and finally given its own CL-Class identity from 1996. The C140 was a genuine four-seat grand tourer with no continuous B-pillars and frameless doors, designed as a flagship statement of Mercedes-Benz engineering. Engine choice ranged from the 4.2-litre and 5.0-litre M119 V8s — the latter widely regarded as one of Mercedes' finest V8s — to the M120 6.0-litre V12, Mercedes' first production V12 and the most powerful Mercedes car engine of its era. Rare diesel variants (S 350 Turbo-D, S 300 Turbo-D) were also available in some markets, though UK uptake was minimal.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes-Benz C140 (coupé) • Predecessor: C126 SEC • Successor: C215 CL-Class • Sibling body styles in this generation: W140 Saloon.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The C140 coupé attracts strong specialist interest in the UK classic car market, with M119 V8 and M120 V12 enquiries dominating pre-purchase inspections and restoration planning [PATTERN DATA].

items.quote: Thin-Data Fallback mode applied — compressed to a single paragraph. Best & Worst Engines: The C140's engine range is one of the most revered in Mercedes history. At the top sits the M119 5.0-litre V8 (500 SEC/S 500/CL 500) — widely considered one of the finest V8s Mercedes ever built, offering a perfect balance of smooth power, robust reliability and accessible running costs. The 4.2-litre M119 (CL 420) delivers similar character with slightly less urge and lower running costs. The M120 6.0-litre V12 (600 SEC/S 600/CL 600) is a masterpiece — 394 bhp, Mercedes' first production V12, and an engine of genuine significance. However, its complexity (two of everything) and specialist parts costs make it significantly more expensive to maintain. Diesel options (OM603/OM606) are rare in the UK and offer strong durability but lack the character expected of a C140. For most buyers, the M119 5.0-litre V8 represents the sweet spot — legendary engine, manageable running costs and the definitive C140 experience. Who it's for: buyers who want the sweet spot between V8 character and classic Mercedes ownership.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Durable Diesel — rare, reliable, but lacks character

economicsRule.title: The C140 rule
economicsRule.text: A £4,000–£6,000 M119 recon against a £10,000–£25,000 C140 sits at 16–60% of the car's value — well within Mercedes-Benz's 70% threshold. Even a £8,000–£12,000 M120 recon on a £15,000–£40,000 V12 example sits at 20–80% — borderline on sub-£20k cars, safe on better examples. The C140's status as a modern classic means values are appreciating — replacement is often justified on cars with strong provenance. Value the car first, but know that the C140 is a car worth investing in.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C140 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: CL 420 (M119 4.2L) — lower entry cost, simpler engine.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C140 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 600 SEC/S 600/CL 600 (M120 V12) — complex V12, expensive parts.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C140 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: 500 SEC/S 500/CL 500 — the definitive C140 experience with manageable running costs.

problems.title: M119/M120 Wiring Harness Degradation (1992–1995)
problems.rootCause: Mercedes used biodegradable insulation for engine wiring harnesses in the early 1990s — the insulation becomes brittle, cracks and exposes copper wiring, causing intermittent faults, engine management issues and, in extreme cases, engine fires. This affects both M119 V8 and M120 V12 engines.
problems.recommendation: If you're buying a 1992–1995 C140, check whether the engine wiring harness has been replaced — a £500–£1,000 preventative replacement is cheap insurance against a £1,800+ electrical failure.

cta.label: Compare C140 wiring harness replacement quotes →

problems.title: M119 Oil Leaks (Valve Cover Gaskets & Distributor Caps)
problems.rootCause: The M119's valve cover gaskets degrade over time, leading to oil leaks that can drip onto the exhaust and cause smoke. Distributor cap and rotor wear is also common on these engines, causing misfires and reduced performance.
problems.recommendation: Have valve cover gaskets inspected at every service — a £400–£800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,000+ reseal that follows a major oil leak.

cta.label: Compare C140 M119 oil leak repair quotes →

problems.title: M120 V12 Ignition Coil & Wiring Harness Issues
problems.rootCause: The M120 V12 has two of everything — two ECUs, two wiring harnesses, two sets of ignition coils. The biodegradable wiring harness issue is particularly acute on V12 cars, and ignition coil failures can cause misfires on individual cylinders.
problems.recommendation: If you're buying a V12 C140, budget for wiring harness inspection and potential replacement — a £700–£1,400 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,500+ replacement that follows an electrical failure.

cta.label: Compare C140 M120 ignition system repair quotes →

problems.title: Vacuum System Failures (Door Closers & Central Locking)
problems.rootCause: The C140's vacuum system powers the door closers, central locking and some other systems — vacuum leaks are common as hoses and actuators age. While not an engine problem per se, it's the most common non-engine issue on the C140.
problems.recommendation: Have the vacuum system checked as part of any classic car inspection — a £250–£500 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £1,000+ replacement that follows a full system failure.

cta.label: Compare C140 vacuum system repair quotes →

replacementCosts.h2: C140 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

items.question: How much does a Mercedes-Benz C140 engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned C140 engine typically costs between £2,500 and £12,000 supply only [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet], depending on engine — the M119 V8 sits at £3,000–£6,000, while the M120 V12 ranges from £6,000–£12,000. Fitting adds 10–22 hours of labour at specialist rates — budget an additional £650–£1,430 for installation. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

items.question: Which Mercedes-Benz C140 engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: The M119 5.0-litre V8 (500 SEC/S 500/CL 500) is widely regarded as one of the most reliable Mercedes V8s ever built — robust construction, proven architecture and excellent longevity. The 4.2-litre M119 is similarly durable. The M120 V12 is also reliable but more complex due to its twin-everything design. Diesel OM603/OM606 variants are exceptionally durable but rare in the UK.

items.question: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz C140?
items.answer: Biodegradable wiring harness degradation on 1992–1995 models (affecting M119 and M120 engines), oil leaks from M119 valve cover gaskets and distributor caps, ignition coil and wiring issues on the M120 V12, and vacuum system failures (door closers, central locking) are the most documented issues. Each requires different preventative strategies.

items.question: Is the Mercedes-Benz C140 reliable?
items.answer: Yes — the C140 is a classic Mercedes built to an extraordinary standard. The M119 V8 is one of the most robust engines Mercedes ever produced, and the M120 V12 is reliable when properly maintained. The key is addressing the wiring harness issue on early cars and keeping on top of oil leaks. A well-maintained C140 can easily exceed 200,000 miles.

items.question: Which Mercedes-Benz C140 engine should I avoid?
items.answer: No engine outright — the C140's engine range is one of the most revered in Mercedes history. However, the M120 V12 is a specialist proposition with significantly higher running costs — parts are expensive and labour-intensive. Diesel variants are reliable but lack the character expected of a flagship coupé. The M119 5.0-litre V8 is the sweet spot.

items.question: How do I know if my C140 engine needs replacing?
items.answer: Wiring faults or intermittent engine management (biodegradable harness), oil leaks from valve covers, rough running or misfires (distributor/coil issues on M120), or blue smoke from the exhaust are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a C140 engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £15,000–£25,000 CL 500, a £5,000 M119 recon is 20–33% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £8,000 example, the same recon is 62% — borderline but still within our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold. For V12 cars, a £10,000 recon on a £20,000–£40,000 example is 25–50% — safe on better cars. The C140's status as a modern classic means values are appreciating.

items.question: How long do Mercedes-Benz C140 engines last?
items.answer: The M119 V8 can comfortably exceed 200,000 miles with proper maintenance — many examples have passed 300,000 miles. The M120 V12 is similarly durable, though wiring harness replacement is essential on early cars. The OM603/OM606 diesels are virtually indestructible with regular servicing.

items.question: What is the difference between the C140, W140 and C215?
items.answer: The C140 is the 2-door coupé (1992–1999, this page), while the W140 is the 4-door saloon (1991–1998). The C215 is the successor CL-Class (1999–2006). All share similar engine families but have different body styles. Costs vary slightly by body style — see each generation's own page for specifics. The C140 was initially badged as SEC, then S-Class Coupé, then CL-Class.

items.question: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz C140 engine code?
items.answer: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on M119/M120/OM603/OM606 units. Tell us your variant and approximate year and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.
